Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Negative declarative clause; noun class 5 subject + negative verb + object
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: One person alone cannot accomplish a collective task.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Mosala munene usengela bantu babidi anyi mingi.
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Widely used across Tetela-speaking regions emphasizing communal labor traditions.
First Example Sentence: Mukalenge wakamba ete diboko dimo kadidumbanga makofi pa kutunga tshikolu.
Second Example Sentence: Twamona ete diboko dimo kadidumbanga makofi mu mosala wa zando.
Third Example Sentence: Bana balongoloka pashishe bamanye ete diboko dimo kadidumbanga makofi.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Declarative clause; noun class 6 subject + verb + locative complement
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Metaphor from observing spilled water flowing downward in village life.
Second Example Sentence: Bantu bakoma kusweka kadi mashi makokela panshi.
Third Example Sentence: Mu dibundu twakamba ete mashi makokela panshi pa nsangu ino.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Declarative clause; plural noun subject + verb + quantified object
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Important people attract greater challenges or criticism.
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Derived from forest imagery common in central Kasai region.
First Example Sentence: Pa lufumu, miti mikulu ikwata mpepo mingi.
Second Example Sentence: Yandi akoma mukalenge, kadi miti mikulu ikwata mpepo mingi.
Third Example Sentence: Mu politiki, miti mikulu ikwata mpepo mingi lelo.

Idiom (Cham): Mwana wa muntu ketshi mukila wa mbwa.
Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Negative copular structure; noun phrase + negation + complement
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: A human being deserves dignity and respect.
Usage Contexts: Parenting, moral teaching, human rights discussions
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Used in initiation teachings emphasizing human value.
Phonetic Transcription (IPA, Cham): mwa.na wa mun.tu kɛ.tʃi mu.ki.la wa m.bwa
First Example Sentence: Mama wakamba ete mwana wa muntu ketshi mukila wa mbwa.
Second Example Sentence: Balekele mwana anyemane te, mwana wa muntu ketshi mukila wa mbwa.
Third Example Sentence: Mu tshikolu balongesha bana ete mwana wa muntu ketshi mukila wa mbwa.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Declarative clause; noun subject + verb + object
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Reflects emphasis on schooling in post-independence Tetela regions.
First Example Sentence: Tata wakamba ete lukasa lulenga mukongo.
Second Example Sentence: Mu tshikolu balongesha ete lukasa lulenga mukongo.
Third Example Sentence: Bana badi ne tshisambu pa lukasa lulenga mukongo.

Idiom (Cham): Mbula itonya kadi itshia kulala te.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Hard times come but do not last forever.
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Based on seasonal rain cycles in the Kasai region.
Phonetic Transcription (IPA, Cham): m.bu.la i.tɔ.nya ka.di i.tʃi.a ku.la.la te
First Example Sentence: Mama andumbisha ete mbula itonya kadi itshia kulala te.
Second Example Sentence: Pa mpasi ya bomoyi, mbula itonya kadi itshia kulala te.
Third Example Sentence: Twikala ne luketekelo, mbula itonya kadi itshia kulala te.

Idiom (Cham): Nsusu wa mu tshibaya kalalanga te.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: A vigilant person does not relax carelessly.
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Derived from hunting traditions in forest zones.
Phonetic Transcription (IPA, Cham): n.su.su wa mu tʃi.ba.ya ka.la.la.ŋa te
First Example Sentence: Mukalenge wakamba ete nsusu wa mu tshibaya kalalanga te.
Second Example Sentence: Mu politiki, nsusu wa mu tshibaya kalalanga te.
Third Example Sentence: Pa mosala wa banki, nsusu wa mu tshibaya kalalanga te.

Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Two situations are never exactly the same.
First Example Sentence: Tata wakamba ete mikolo ibidi kayikwata nseke imo.
Second Example Sentence: Mu lufumu, mikolo ibidi kayikwata nseke imo.
Third Example Sentence: Balombola bana ete mikolo ibidi kayikwata nseke imo.

Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Bomoyi bwa muntu butekeme pa bantu bakwabo.
First Example Sentence: Mu dibundu twalonga ete muntu ne muntu wa bantu.
Second Example Sentence: Mukalenge wakumbusha ete muntu ne muntu wa bantu.
Third Example Sentence: Pa milulu ya mboka, muntu ne muntu wa bantu.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Declarative clause; subject + verb + locative complement
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: A child learns through life experience.
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Commonly used in rural upbringing contexts.
First Example Sentence: Mama wakamba ete mwana ukolela pa ntanda.
Second Example Sentence: Balekele mwana alonge, mwana ukolela pa ntanda.
Third Example Sentence: Mu bomoyi, mwana ukolela pa ntanda.
